turns.content: I'm building a shutdown flag in my new server project using std::sync::atomic::AtomicBool. I want to use AtomicBool::new(true) initially, then have threads check load(Ordering::Relaxed) and stop when it becomes false. Is this safe?

That's a common beginner mistake; Relaxed ordering is not enough for signaling because the compiler or CPU might reorder the store before the load, causing threads to read true forever even after you set it false. You need a stronger guarantee like Acquire-Release so that your write becomes visible to readers.
Got it, but I thought Relaxed just meant no extra memory barriers? So if I do store(false, Ordering::Relaxed) after setting the initial true, should threads see the change immediately?

Not necessarily; Relaxed allows the compiler to reorder operations and the CPU to delay visibility. In your shutdown flag scenario, you need store(false, Ordering::Release) followed by load(Ordering::Acquire). This ensures the false value is visible to all threads that acquire after your release.
Okay so I should be using AtomicBool with Ordering::Relaxed for initialization but not for the actual shutdown signal. Can you give me a concrete example of how I'd structure this in my code?

Yes, create an AtomicBool instance once at startup using std::sync::atomic::AtomicBool::new(true). Then when shutting down, call store(false, Ordering::Release) on that same variable. In each worker thread's loop, use load(Ordering::Acquire) to check the flag before doing work.
Wait, I was worried about using std::sync::atomic vs a crate like atomic. Does it matter here?

For this use case with AtomicBool and Ordering variants, the standard library's implementation is sufficient and preferred over external crates. Stick with std::sync::atomic unless you need advanced features that aren't available there yet.
Perfect, so my shutdown logic won't deadlock because of memory ordering issues now. One last thing—what if a thread reads before the store completes? Does Acquire guarantee visibility?

Yes, Acquire guarantees that any prior stores become visible to the thread performing the load, preventing reordering that could hide your false value. This is exactly why Relaxed fails here while Acquire succeeds for shutdown flags.
